paper_title: CARVE: Certified Affordable Repair of Vetoed Maneuvers via Envelopes for Interactive Driving

sections.title: Abstract
sections.content: Interactive driving exposes a failure mode that is easy to miss in rule-aware autonomous-driving stacks: a hard-rule margin can be negative for an ego candidate even though a small lawful accommodation by a non-priority agent would restore feasibility. Existing rulebooks, shields, and reachability filters are strong at vetoing unsafe actions, while prediction-based planners model likely responses. Neither returns a runtime proof object that states which bounded multi-agent edit repairs the maneuver, who owns the edit, whether the request is right-of-way affordable, and what ego fallback remains if the request is not observed. We formulate this missing object as *interactive repair certification* and introduce *CARVE*, a prediction-free certificate layer over a finite lattice of ego-owned and agent-owned tactical operators. Agent-owned requests are admissible only inside \(B_j(s) = β(π_j)α_j^{\max}(s)\), a cooperation envelope that separates kinematic reachability from normative priority. The resulting certificate records the binding rule, repair category, repair set, responsibility-weighted cost split, and fallback. On 589 Lanelet2-geometry-grounded INTERACTION replay episodes, CARVE-Greedy accepts 98.64% of initially vetoed maneuvers and recovers 370/378 human-resolved false vetoes, while preserving 589/589 right-of-way respect, zero priority-agent false positives, and 400/400 negative-stress vetoes. We prove certificate soundness, structural right-of-way respect, exact finite-lattice minimality, fallback contingency, and blame-consistency conditions. CARVE does not predict or require another driver's compliance; it certifies whether a proposed interaction is bounded, attributable, and normatively admissible under declared assumptions.

Existing rulebooks, shields, and reachability filters are strong at vetoing unsafe actions, while prediction-based planners model likely responses. Neither returns runtime proof object that states which bounded multi-agent edit repairs the maneuver, who owns the edit, whether the request is right-of-way affordable, and what ego fallback remains if the request is not observed. We formulate this missing object as interactive repair certification and introduce CARVE, prediction-free certificate layer over finite lattice of egoowned and agent-owned tactical operators. Agent-owned re- (s), quests are admissible only inside Bj(s) = β(πj)αmax cooperation envelope that separates kinematic reachability from normative priority. The resulting certificate records the binding rule, repair category, repair set, responsibilityweighted cost split, and fallback. On 589 Lanelet2-geometrygrounded INTERACTION replay episodes, CARVE-Greedy accepts 98.64% of initially vetoed maneuvers and recovers 370/378 human-resolved false vetoes, while preserving 589/589 right-of-way respect, zero priority-agent false positives, and 400/400 negative-stress vetoes. We prove certificate soundness, structural right-of-way respect, exact finite-lattice minimality, fallback contingency, and blame-consistency conditions. CARVE does not predict or require another drivers compliance; it certifies whether proposed interaction is bounded, attributable, and normatively admissible under declared assumptions.

sections.title: Introduction
sections.content: Autonomous-driving systems have made rapid progress in perception, forecasting, and motion generation, but their deployment still depends on more basic property: the vehicle must act safely, legally, and transparently in interactive traffic (Koopman and Wagner 2017). Rulebooks and temporal-logic planners specify priority among rules (Censi et al. 2019; Wongpiromsarn, Topcu, and Murray 2012; Tumova et al. 2013); RSS-style models define responsibility and duties of care (Shalev-Shwartz, Shammah, and Shashua 2017); and reachability tools bound what agents can safely do (Althoff and Dolan 2014; Althoff and Magdici 2016). These lines of work give modern AV stacks powerful veto mechanisms. The less studied question is what should happen after veto when the scene is interactive and the violation is repairable. This paper studies the false-veto problem in rule-aware interactive driving. candidate merge, roundabout entry, or unsignalized-intersection crossing may violate hard gap margin under its current timing. binary rule gate must reject it. Yet human traffic often resolves exactly such cases through small, lawful accommodations: the ego waits slightly, nonpriority vehicle yields within its comfortable deceleration budget, or both parties make minor tactical edit. Treating the first negative margin as terminal discards these recoverable interactions and biases the planner toward unnecessary conservatism. Existing alternatives leave critical gap. Ego-only trajectory repair can make the ego wait or decelerate, but it cannot certify bounded request to another agent. Interaction-aware planners can predict that another driver will yield, but then the safety argument depends on the correctness of behavior model. Hard-prune rulebooks give clear vetoes, but they do not produce positive witness explaining why repaired maneuver is admissible. What is missing is runtime object that answers four questions together: which rule binds, which finite edit repairs it, who owns the edit, and whether every requested accommodation is affordable under right-of-way. We call this object an interactive repair certificate. certificate is not predicted trajectory and not learned confidence score. It is compact proof object that states the binding rule, the selected repair operators, their owners and costs, the right-of-way-scaled request bounds, and the ego fallback available if an elicited request is not observed. This formulation turns interactive recovery into an auditable AI decision problem: learned or rule-based planners may propose maneuvers, while the certificate layer decides whether the proposed interaction has bounded, attributable, normatively admissible repair witness. CARVE instantiates this idea as prediction-free repair layer over finite multi-owner tactical lattice. Its key mech- (s), anism is cooperation envelope Bj(s) = β(πj)αmax where αmax (s) is kinematic accommodation bound and β(πj) scales that bound by the agents right-of-way status. Thus non-priority agent may be asked for small bounded yield, while priority agent receives no nonzero request. Because certificate validity depends on the declared envelope and lattice rather than on learned response model, CARVE j can recover false vetoes without assuming another driver will comply. Our contributions are: We identify false-veto recovery as an interactive certification problem rather than trajectory-prediction problem, and formalize repair certificates over finite multi-owner operator lattice. We introduce right-of-way-scaled cooperation envelope Bj = β(πj)αmax , which cleanly separates physical reachability from normative admissibility and blocks requests to priority agents. We give exact and greedy certificate procedures with soundness, finite-lattice minimality, structural right-ofway respect, fallback contingency, and multi-agent blameconsistency conditions. We evaluate CARVE on Lanelet2-geometry-grounded INTERACTION replay episodes with ablations, negative stress tests, integrity checks, and synthetic multi-agent blame-consistency stress, showing high false-veto recovery without priority-agent false positives.

sections.title: 2 Related Work
sections.content: Rule-aware planning and safety filters. Rulebooks, temporal logic, and minimum-violation synthesis reason about prioritized specifications (Censi et al. 2019; Wongpiromsarn, Topcu, and Murray 2012; Tumova et al. 2013). Controlbarrier-function filters and shielding methods provide runtime safety interventions in continuous control or reinforcement learning (Ames et al. 2017; Alshiekh et al. 2018). CARVE is complementary: it operates at the tactical decision layer and emits human-readable multi-agent certificate, including repair ownership and right-of-way affordability, rather than continuous control correction alone. Trajectory repair and reachability. Reachability analysis provides conservative envelopes for traffic participants (Althoff and Dolan 2014; Althoff and Magdici 2016), and CommonRoad supplies reproducible motion-planning benchmarks (Althoff, Koschi, and Manzinger 2017). The closest ego-only repair work uses SMT and reachability to repair violating ego trajectory (Lin et al. 2024). CARVE instead searches discrete tactical lattice, includes agent-owned bounded accommodations, and allocates repair burden across agents. Prediction and interaction-aware planning. Motion forecasting has progressed from social recurrent models (Alahi et al. 2016; Deo and Trivedi 2018) to map-aware multimodal models (Salzmann et al. 2020; Chai et al. 2020; Gao et al. 2020). Game-theoretic and interaction-aware planners model how an ego action affects other agents (Sadigh et al. 2016; Fisac et al. 2019; Schwarting et al. 2019; FridovichKeil et al. 2020; Hubmann et al. 2018); interaction-aware trajectory repair explicitly predicts responses (Wang, Lin, and Althoff 2024). CARVE does not replace these models. It audits candidate maneuver and certifies bounded requests without assuming that response model is correct. Family Cert. Bounded RoW Pred.-free Blame Rulebook / hard gate Ego trajectory repair Interaction prediction CBF / shielding CARVE partial Table 1: Capability comparison. Bounded denotes explicit certification of agent-owned requests inside cooperation envelope. Recent AAAI decision-making context. Recent AAAI work studies regulation-aware driving decisions, searchbased vehicle planning, and safety-critical scenario generation (Cai et al. 2026; Nachkov et al. 2026; Xu et al. 2025). CARVE is complementary: it does not generate trajectories or scenarios, but certifies whether proposed interactive maneuver has bounded, attributable, right-of-way-affordable repair witness. Datasets and map grounding. Large datasets support perception and forecasting (Caesar et al. 2020; Chang et al. 2019; Sun et al. 2020; Ettinger et al. 2021). Drone-based trajectory datasets provide clean kinematics (Krajewski et al. 2018; Bock et al. 2020). INTERACTION targets adversarial and cooperative driving with semantic maps (Zhan et al. 2019); Lanelet2 provides the map abstraction (Poggenhans et al. 2018). We use INTERACTION as replay test set only; CARVE learns no parameters from it.

sections.title:
3 Problem Formulation
Let s be a scene with ego state, agents, map context, and right-
of-way roles πj. Let m be a candidate maneuver and H =
{h1, . . . , hL} a prioritized hard-rule prefix. Each rule returns
a margin gℓ(m, s); m is hard feasible iff all margins are
nonnegative. We use gap and time-to-conflict margins related
to TTC and car-following safety primitives (Minderhoud and
Bovy 2001; Treiber, Hennecke, and Helbing 2000; Kesting,
Treiber, and Helbing 2010).
sections.content: a is tuple lattice. An operator Operator = (owner, type, Θo, ρo, go). The owner is ego or specific agent j; Θo is finite parameter grid; ρo(θ) 0 is normalized effort; and go(θ) 0 is the margin gain on the binding rule. repair set is finite set of operator-parameter assignments. The lattice is ordered by set inclusion over assignments; exact search finds minimum-cost feasible element of this finite lattice. Objective and affordability. For repair set A, CARVE minimizes Φ(A) = (cid:88) ρo(θ) + (o,θ)Aego (cid:88) w(πj) (cid:88) ρo(θ), (1) (o,θ)Aj where ρo is normalized operator effort and w(πj) is the responsibility weight used for blame ordering. Feasibility, however, is checked in the units of the owner. Ego edits are charged to the normalized effort budget Bego. The two Figure 1: Overview. CARVE converts an initially infeasible interactive candidate into finite repair search and certificate. Unlike prediction-based repair, CARVE certifies bounded requests and an ego fallback; unlike hard-prune rulebooks, it can recover false vetoes through right-of-way-affordable interaction. budgets are not compared to each other. If one or more agentowned requests target agent j, their total positive speedreduction magnitude is in m/s and must satisfy Bj(s) = β(πj)αmax 0 Bj(s), (s). (2) Here β(πj) [0, 1] is dimensionless: priority, equal-duty, and yielding agents use 0, 0.5, 0.8, respectively. This table is not learned or fit to maximize recovery; it encodes an ordinal duty structure in which priority agents receive zero request budget, equal-duty agents receive conservative partial envelope, and yielding agents receive larger but still subreachability envelope. The kinematic bound is the closedform speed-reduction inner bound αmax (s) = min(amin T, vj2), (3) with horizon = 5 in the replay protocol. Thus Bj is in m/s, and the affordability screen compares it to the requested accommodation magnitude. The responsibilityweighted cost in Φ is used for minimality and blame ordering, not as the agent-side feasibility unit. This separation is why tightening or loosening β changes recovery and CPA while structural priority violations remain impossible when priority agents keep β = 0. Elicitation semantics. CARVE does not assume communication channel. certificate may be implemented by V2X, user-interface advisory, or monitored hypothesis in the planner. If the requested accommodation is not observed, the ego executes or replans around the fallback. The certificate asserts admissibility of request, not compliance. Certificate. certificate is = (κ, h, A, ρego, {ρj}, Afb), where κ is category, is the binding rule, is the selected repair, ρego, {ρj} are cost allocations, and Afb is an ego-only contingency when available.

sections.title:
5 Guarantees
All statements are with respect to the configured finite lat-
tice, declared rules, and declared envelopes. Proofs and as-
sumption audits are in the supplement. The assumptions are
finite operator grids, deterministic declared margins, mono-
tone operator gains, admissible exact-search lower bounds,
unit-consistent envelopes, and exchangeability for the blame
theorem.
Theorem 1 (Certificate soundness) If CARVE returns an
accepting certificate with repair A⋆, then mA⋆ satisfies ev-
ery rule in H, ego cost is within Bego, and every requested
accommodation satisfies 0 ≤ ∆j ≤ Bj(s).
sections.content: Figure 2: Certificate anatomy. Blue elements denote ego-owned edits; teal elements denote agent-owned accommodation requests. The guarantee boundary is hard-rule feasibility plus 0 Bj(s), not prediction of another drivers compliance. This holds because acceptance is gated by recomputing all hard margins and the affordability predicate on the selected set. Theorem 2 (Structural right-of-way respect) If agent is labeled priority, then an accepting certificate contains no positive accommodation request owned by j. The mechanism is structural: β(πj) = 0 implies Bj = 0, so any positive request fails the affordability screen. Theorem 3 (Finite-lattice minimality) Exact CARVE returns minimum-Φ affordable repair over the finite operator lattice, or reports that no affordable feasible repair exists. The lower bound never overestimates the remaining completion cost; therefore pruned node cannot contain better incumbent. Theorem 4 (Fallback contingency boundary) For elicited or joint certificates with an ego fallback, ego has an executable contingency if accommodation is not observed. the the requested The guarantee is runtime boundary, not compliance prediction. Theorem 5 (Blame consistency) For distinct-duty agents with exchangeable accommodation operators and sufficient capacity, exact CARVE cannot allocate more raw accommodation magnitude to lower-duty agent than to higher-duty agent. Here exchangeable means that equal raw speed-reduction magnitudes have the same margin-channel effect for the compared agents. Equal-duty agents are intentionally unordered. The theorem orders raw accommodation magnitude, not weighted cost. The result follows from swap argument: moving burden from lower-duty, higher-weight agent to higher-duty, lower-weight agent lowers Φ, contradicting exact minimality. Audit stage Count Purpose Lanelet2-grounded replay set Initial hard-gate vetoes Ego-only repair accepted Human-resolved ego-only vetoes CARVE accepted / refused 589 589 211 378 581 / 8 satisfied-onset final evaluation set no cases floor comparator FVRR denominator certificate outcome Table 2: Auditable replay funnel after deterministic filters. Raw-data regeneration, thresholds, and schema are in the supplement and code artifact.

sections.title: 6 Evaluation Protocol
sections.content: Episode mining. We mine replay episodes using INTERACTION trajectories and Lanelet2 map geometry. When regulatory right-of-way metadata is available, we record it explicitly; otherwise, we assign geometry-derived proxy label and report that source separately. Inclusion requires: (i) an ego-agent conflict near Lanelet2 conflict point; (ii) an ego-unilateral hard gate veto at onset; (iii) observed human resolution later in the window; and (iv) non-fragmented tracks with unambiguous intent. No learning or tuning is performed on INTERACTION. is the Baselines. HardPrune terminal-veto baseline. EgoOnly-Greedy and EgoOnly-Exact use the same rules, costs, parameter grids, and search procedures as CARVE, with only agent-owned operators removed. UniversalYieldUpperBound is diagnostic, not deployable method: it sets all agents to willing yielders and shows the unconstrained cooperative ceiling after removing normative right-of-way constraints. AlphaOnly-CARVE removes β from Bj, and NoElicit removes agent-owned operators. Metrics. Accept is computed over all 589 replay episodes. FVRR is false-veto recovery over the 378 human-resolved ego-only vetoes. CPA is an envelope diagnostic, not return satisfied certificate with empty repair Algorithm 1 CARVE Decision Procedure 1: Input: maneuver m, scene s, rules H, operators O, mode 2: if gℓ(m, s) 0 for all hℓ then 3: 4: end if 5: highest-priority rule with negative margin 6: gh (m, s); 7: for each operator do 8: if θ Θo with positive gain and possible affordability then add feasible assignments (o, θ) to 9: end if 10: 11: end for 12: if mode is exact then 13: 14: run branch-and-bound over nodes (A, drem, Φ) lower bound LB fractional cheapest-margin cover of drem prune nodes with Φ + LB no better than incumbent greedily add affordable assignment maximizing g/ρ 15: 16: else 17: 18: end if 19: if no affordable feasible repair is found then 20: return over-budget if feasibility appears only without budgets, else non-repairable 21: end if 22: compute best ego-only fallback Afb, if any 23: return category, A, cost split, and Afb prediction-accuracy metric: for eligible elicited or joint certificates with measurable realized response, CPA is one when the observed accommodation is inside Bj. BCR is computed over eligible certificates with nonzero agent-side cost allocations. Fallback is reported over elicited or joint certificates where the finite lattice also contains an ego-only contingency. RHA is repair-human agreement; it is diagnostic only because CARVE searches for minimal certificates rather than imitating human repair magnitudes.

sections.title:
7 Results
Table 3 gives the central contrast. Ego-only repair, with iden-
tical rules, costs, grids, and search but with agent-owned op-
erators removed, accepts only 35.82% of the mined interac-
tive conflicts. CARVE-Greedy accepts 98.64% and recovers
370/378 false vetoes, showing that the missing capacity is
lawful multi-owner cooperation rather than a weaker search
configuration. This recovery does not come from ignoring
normative priority: RoW-respect is 589/589, priority false
positives are zero, and BCR is 574/574. The diagnostic up-
per bound accepts every case only by allowing universal
yielding; AlphaOnly also reaches 100% FVRR but produces
14 priority-agent false positives.
sections.content: Sensitivity to hand-designed parameters. The operator coefficients are declared protocol parameters, not learned. To test whether the result is tuning artifact, we sweep gain, ego-cost, agent-cost, TTC-threshold, and β-table families. Across all non-priority envelope sweeps, RoW-respect reFigure 3: Replay evaluation and ablation evidence. The main certificate metrics are FVRR, RoW-respect, and BCR; CPA(Bj) is an envelope diagnostic; RHA is behavior diagnostic. Alpha-only improves raw recovery but violates priority; negative stress and synthetic BCR stress audit safety and responsibility properties. mains 100.00% and priority false positives remain zero. strict β table lowers FVRR to 95.50% and CPA to 48.57%; permissive table keeps FVRR at 97.88% and raises CPA to 79.34%; AlphaOnly raises CPA further but creates 14 priority false positives. Thus the key invariant comes from the structural priority envelope, not tuned coefficient. Failure analysis. The eight unrecovered false vetoes are categorized as repairable only outside the declared budgets. They are therefore not silent failures: CARVE refuses to certify repairs that require priority yielding or exceed Bj. CPA failures similarly indicate that observed human accommodation sometimes exceeds the conservative normative envelope, not that the certificate predicted the wrong behavior. higher CPA can be obtained by loosening the envelope, but that is not win when it admits priority requests. RHA separates type from magnitude: broad repair type matches 359/581 cases, while full type-plus-magnitude RHA is 164/581, consistent with humans often choosing larger, later, or more comfortable maneuvers than minimal certificate. The default certificate categories are 7 ego-only, 563 elicited, 11 joint, and 8 over-budget refusals. Fallback coverage is not reMethod Accept FVRR RoW-resp. Prio. Values are percentages except falsepositive counts and latency. n/a means the metric is undefined for that comparator; the upper-bound row is an unconstrained diagnostic, not deployable baseline. For CARVE-Greedy, FVRR is 370/378, RoW-respect is 589/589, BCR is 574/574, and negative-stress veto is 400/400. Latency was measured on 4-core/8-thread laptop CPU. Row source Claim status all-way stop geometry-arrival proxy geometry-equal proxy explicit regulatory RoW partial regulatory RoW 143 198 204 1 43 stop-type metadata proxy label proxy label explicit metadata partial metadata Table 4: Right-of-way source stratification. All episodes are Lanelet2-geometry grounded; proxy rows are not claimed as explicit regulatory labels. quired for every accepted certificate; for 204/574 elicited or joint certificates an ego-only contingency exists, otherwise runtime commitment must monitor and recertify. Row-source and multi-agent audits. The replay set spans 11 INTERACTION locations. We keep row-source labels separate because many labels are geometry-derived proxies, not explicit regulatory right-of-way. Naturalistic multi-agent evidence is modest: 45 two-agent and 2 three-agent conflict scenes yield 24 eligible real pairwise BCR checks. We therefore separately use synthetic distinct-duty stress set, which passes 648/648 BCR scenes and 2304/2304 pairwise checks. The synthetic set validates the formal property; it is not presented as naturalistic scale. Negative and integrity checks. Negative stress consists of 200 unrepairable collision cases and 200 priority-overbudget cases where the only feasible repair would require nonzero request to priority agent with β = 0. CARVE vetoes 400/400. Integrity scripts replay saved decisions and find zero main-set or negative-set failures. The package includes 17 unit tests covering affordability units, right-of-way invariants, branch-and-bound admissibility, operator monotonicity, and Lanelet2 parser invariants.

sections.title:
8 Discussion and Limitations
CARVE is a certification layer, not a full AV stack. It can
wrap learned behavior planners, trajectory generators, or
rule-based candidate generators by auditing proposed ma-
neuvers. This supports trustworthy and explainable AI: black-
box components may propose, while CARVE returns an au-
ditable decision object naming the binding rule, responsible
sections.content: party, request bound, and fallback. The guarantees are relative to finite tactical lattice, declared margins, and conservative envelopes. They do not imply global continuous optimality, closed-loop safety under unmodeled rules, or another drivers compliance. Open-loop replay evaluates certificate logic against observed human resolutions; this is the appropriate first test for the certificate logic, but it does not measure closed-loop social feedback. Closed-loop validation with reactive agents in CommonRoad or CARLA is future work. Only small subset of the replay episodes contains explicit regulatory RoW metadata; the rest are reported as all-way-stop metadata or geometryderived proxies. The current lattice targets one to three conflict agents; dense scenes require hierarchical conflict clustering, and repairs outside the finite tactical ontology are out of scope. The evaluation does not claim that CARVE outperforms all interaction-aware planners. UniversalYield is an upper bound, not an official external implementation. The comparison isolates different question: whether an initially vetoed maneuver admits bounded, attributable, right-ofway-respecting repair certificate.

sections.title:
9 Conclusion
We introduced CARVE, a certificate-generating repair layer
for interactive driving. CARVE turns infeasibility into a min-
imal, affordable, and blame-consistent repair object without
predicting another agent’s response. On INTERACTION re-
play episodes it recovers most human-resolved false vetoes
while preserving structural right-of-way respect and vetoing
unsafe stress cases. This makes repairability a first-class, au-
ditable decision object: not merely whether a maneuver is
allowed, but whose bounded action would make it allowed
and under what normative envelope.
